questions.question:
Which of the following is/are Bottom-Up Parser(s)?
A. Shift-reduce Parser
B. Predictive Parser
C. LL Parser
D. LR Parser
questions.to_find: Identify which of the given parsers are bottom-up parsers

understand.plain: Parsers can be classified as top-down (build parse tree from root to leaves) or bottom-up (build parse tree from leaves to root). Bottom-up parsers start with the input string and reduce it to the start symbol by reversing productions. Top-down parsers start from the start symbol and try to match the input. We need to identify which of the four given parsers belong to the bottom-up category.

keywords.explain: A parser that starts from the input tokens (leaves) and works upward toward the start symbol by applying reductions. It builds the parse tree bottom-up.
keywords.example: LR parsers reduce 'id + id' by first making id a factor, then factor an expression, etc.

keywords.explain: A bottom-up parsing technique that uses a stack. It either shifts (pushes) the next input symbol onto the stack or reduces (replaces) a string on top of the stack using a grammar production.
keywords.example: For input 'id+id', it shifts 'id', reduces to E, shifts '+', shifts 'id', reduces to E, then reduces E+E to E.

keywords.explain: A type of shift-reduce (bottom-up) parser. LR stands for Left-to-right scanning, Rightmost derivation in reverse. Includes SLR, LALR, and Canonical LR parsers.
keywords.example: LR(0), SLR(1), LALR(1), and CLR(1) are all LR parsers — all bottom-up.

keywords.explain: A top-down parser that reads input Left-to-right and produces a Leftmost derivation. It predicts which production to use based on lookahead tokens.
keywords.example: LL(1) parser uses a predictive parsing table with 1 token of lookahead.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="220" viewBox="0 0 480 220" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="210" height="180" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="115" y="38" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Top-Down</text><rect x="25" y="48" width="180" height="40" rx="6" class="bg" style="stroke:#B4B2A9;"/><text x="115" y="73" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Predictive Parser</text><rect x="25" y="96" width="180" height="40" rx="6" class="bg" style="stroke:#B4B2A9;"/><text x="115" y="121" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">LL(1) Parser</text><rect x="25" y="144" width="180" height="36" rx="6" class="bg" style="stroke:#B4B2A9;"/><text x="115" y="167" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Recursive Descent</text><rect x="250" y="10" width="210" height="180" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="355" y="38" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Bottom-Up</text><rect x="265" y="48" width="180" height="40" rx="6" style="fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);stroke:#1D9E75;"/><text x="355" y="73" text-anchor="middle" class="sub" style="fill:white;">Shift-Reduce Parser</text><rect x="265" y="96" width="180" height="40" rx="6" style="fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);stroke:#1D9E75;"/><text x="355" y="121" text-anchor="middle" class="sub" style="fill:white;">LR Parser (SLR/LALR/CLR)</text><rect x="265" y="144" width="180" height="36" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="355" y="167" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Operator Precedence</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes: Top-Down parsers (Predictive, LL(1), Recursive Descent) on the left; Bottom-Up parsers (Shift-Reduce, LR, Operator Precedence) on the right, with Bottom-Up highlighted.

given.aim: Identify all bottom-up parsers from the four options given

terms.meaning: A bottom-up parsing strategy using a stack with shift and reduce operations
terms.example: Used in LR parsing: push tokens, reduce by productions
terms.connects: Directly a bottom-up parser — correct answer

terms.meaning: A top-down parser that predicts the production to use without backtracking
Top-down parser: Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation
terms.example: LL(1) uses 1 token lookahead and a predictive parsing table

terms.meaning: Bottom-up parser: Left-to-right scan, Rightmost derivation in reverse
terms.example: SLR(1), LALR(1), CLR(1) are all LR parsers
terms.connects: LR is a shift-reduce parser — bottom-up — correct answer

given.plan: Recall the two main categories of parsers: top-down (LL, Predictive, Recursive Descent) and bottom-up (LR, Shift-Reduce, Operator Precedence). Classify each option accordingly and select those that are bottom-up.

steps.title: Classify top-down parsers
steps.apply: Predictive Parser (option B) and LL Parser (option C) are both top-down parsers. In top-down parsing, derivation begins from the start symbol and proceeds toward the leaves. LL parsers produce leftmost derivation. These are NOT bottom-up parsers.
steps.note: LL(1) is also known as a predictive parser — both B and C are top-down.

steps.title: Classify bottom-up parsers
steps.apply: Shift-Reduce Parser (option A) is the fundamental bottom-up parsing technique — it maintains a stack and either shifts the next input onto the stack or reduces the top of the stack using a production. LR Parser (option D) is a specific family of shift-reduce parsers (SLR, LALR, CLR). Both A and D are bottom-up parsers.
steps.note: All LR parsers (SLR, LALR, CLR) are shift-reduce parsers and hence bottom-up. So A and D are both correct.

solution.result: Options A (Shift-reduce Parser) and D (LR Parser) are Bottom-Up Parsers.

questions.formula_note: Classification of parsers: Top-Down (LL, Predictive, Recursive Descent) vs Bottom-Up (LR, Shift-Reduce, Operator Precedence). LR parsers are a subset of shift-reduce parsers.

questions.question:
Consider the following context-free grammar where the start symbol is S and the set of terminals is {a, b, c, $}:

S -> AaAb | BbBa
A -> cS | epsilon
B -> epsilon | e

The following is a partially-filled LL(1) parsing table.

        a          b          c          $
S    S->AaAb    S->BbBa      (1)       blank
A    A->eps      (2)      A->cS       blank
B      (3)      B->eps      (4)       blank

Which one of the following options represents the CORRECT combination for the numbered cells in the parsing table?
Note: In the options, 'blank' denotes that the corresponding cell is empty.

A. (1) S->AaAb (2) A->eps (3) B->eps (4) blank
B. (1) S->BbBa (2) A->eps (3) B->eps (4) blank
C. (1) S->AaAb (2) A->cS  (3) blank  (4) B->eps
D. (1) S->AaAb (2) A->eps (3) blank  (4) blank
questions.to_find: The correct entries for cells (1), (2), (3), (4) in the LL(1) parsing table

understand.plain: In LL(1) parsing, the table entry M[A, t] tells which production to use when non-terminal A is on the stack top and terminal t is the current input. An entry M[A, t] = A -> alpha is added when t is in FIRST(alpha), or when epsilon is in FIRST(alpha) and t is in FOLLOW(A). We need to compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets to fill in cells (1) through (4).

keywords.explain: FIRST(alpha) is the set of terminals that can appear as the first symbol in strings derived from alpha. If alpha can derive epsilon, then epsilon is also in FIRST(alpha).
keywords.example: For A -> cS | epsilon: FIRST(A) = {c, epsilon}

keywords.explain: FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of A in some sentential form. Used to decide when to apply epsilon-productions.
keywords.example: If S -> AaAb, then 'a' is in FOLLOW(A) at the first position, and 'b' is in FOLLOW(A) at the second position.

keywords.explain: A 2D table M[non-terminal, terminal] that drives the LL(1) parser. Entry M[A,a] = production means 'use this production when A is on stack and a is input'.
keywords.example: M[S, a] = S->AaAb means: when S is on stack and 'a' is input, expand S using S->AaAb.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="200" viewBox="0 0 480 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="460" height="30" class="hi"/><text x="60" y="30" class="tag ink">NT</text><text x="130" y="30" class="tag ink">a</text><text x="210" y="30" class="tag ink">b</text><text x="290" y="30" class="tag ink">c</text><text x="370" y="30" class="tag ink">$</text><rect x="10" y="40" width="460" height="40" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="65" class="sub ink">S</text><text x="115" y="65" class="sub ink">AaAb</text><text x="195" y="65" class="sub ink">BbBa</text><text x="275" y="65" class="sub ink" style="fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);font-weight:700;">(1)?</text><text x="360" y="65" class="sub mut">blank</text><rect x="10" y="80" width="460" height="40" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="105" class="sub ink">A</text><text x="115" y="105" class="sub ink">eps</text><text x="275" y="105" class="sub ink" style="fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);font-weight:700;">(2)?</text><text x="195" y="105" class="sub ink" style="fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);font-weight:700;">(2)?</text><text x="275" y="105" class="sub ink">cS</text><text x="360" y="105" class="sub mut">blank</text><rect x="10" y="120" width="460" height="40" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="145" class="sub ink">B</text><text x="115" y="145" class="sub ink" style="fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);font-weight:700;">(3)?</text><text x="195" y="145" class="sub ink">eps</text><text x="275" y="145" class="sub ink" style="fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);font-weight:700;">(4)?</text><text x="360" y="145" class="sub mut">blank</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: LL(1) parsing table with rows S, A, B and columns a, b, c, $. Cells (1), (2), (3), (4) are the unknown entries to be determined.

given.aim: Fill cells (1), (2), (3), (4) in the LL(1) parsing table for the given grammar

terms.meaning: S->AaAb | BbBa, A->cS | epsilon, B->epsilon | e (but image shows B->epsilon only relevant here)
terms.example: S has two productions; A and B can derive epsilon
terms.connects: Need FIRST and FOLLOW for each to fill the table

terms.meaning: Since A can derive epsilon, FIRST(AaAb) includes FIRST(A) minus epsilon, plus {a} = {c, a}
terms.example: FIRST(A) = {c, epsilon}, so FIRST(AaAb) = {c} union {a} = {a, c}

terms.meaning: Since B can derive epsilon, FIRST(BbBa) includes FIRST(B) minus epsilon, plus {b} = {b}
terms.connects: M[S,b] = S->BbBa (given), no entry for S in column c from this production

terms.meaning: FOLLOW(A): from S->AaAb, first A has 'a' after it, second A has 'b' after it. So FOLLOW(A) = {a, b}. FOLLOW(B): from S->BbBa, first B has 'b', second B has 'a'. So FOLLOW(B) = {a, b}.
terms.example: FOLLOW(A) = {a, b}, used for A->epsilon entries

given.plan: Compute FIRST sets for all productions, then FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals. Use rule: M[A,t] = A->alpha if t in FIRST(alpha); M[A,t] = A->alpha if epsilon in FIRST(alpha) and t in FOLLOW(A). Determine entries for cells (1)-(4).

steps.title: Compute FIRST sets
steps.formula_raw: FIRST(alpha): set of terminals that begin strings derived from alpha
steps.note: Since FIRST(AaAb) contains 'c', cell (1) = M[S,c] = S->AaAb.

steps.title: Compute FOLLOW(A)
steps.formula_raw: FOLLOW(A): terminals/$ that follow A in any sentential form
steps.apply:
From S -> AaAb:
  First occurrence of A: 'a' follows A, so 'a' in FOLLOW(A)
  Second occurrence of A: 'b' follows A, so 'b' in FOLLOW(A)
From A -> cS: S is followed by whatever follows A, but we need FOLLOW(S) first.
FOLLOW(S) = {$} (S is start symbol)
Also from S -> AaAb and S -> BbBa: these don't add more to FOLLOW(A).
So FOLLOW(A) = {a, b}.

For A->epsilon: M[A,t] = A->epsilon for each t in FOLLOW(A) = {a, b}.
M[A,a] = A->epsilon (already given in table).
Cell (2) = M[A,b] = A->epsilon.
steps.note: A->epsilon is used when input is in FOLLOW(A), i.e., {a, b}.

steps.title: Compute FOLLOW(B) and fill cell (3)
steps.formula_raw: FOLLOW(B): terminals that follow B in any sentential form
steps.apply:
From S -> BbBa:
  First B: 'b' follows B, so 'b' in FOLLOW(B)
  Second B: 'a' follows B, so 'a' in FOLLOW(B)
So FOLLOW(B) = {a, b}.

For B->epsilon: M[B,t] = B->epsilon for each t in FOLLOW(B).
M[B,b] = B->epsilon (already given in table).
Cell (3) = M[B,a] = B->epsilon.
steps.note: B can only derive epsilon in this grammar, so its epsilon production is used at its FOLLOW positions.

steps.title: Determine cell (4) = M[B, c]
steps.apply:
B has only one production: B->epsilon.
For B->epsilon, the table entry is placed at FOLLOW(B) = {a, b}.
'c' is NOT in FOLLOW(B).
Also 'c' is not in FIRST(B) (which is just {epsilon}).
So cell (4) = M[B, c] = blank (error entry).
steps.note: If 'c' appears as input when B is on the stack, it is a parse error — the cell is blank.

steps.title: Summary of cells
steps.apply:
(1) M[S,c] = S->AaAb
(2) M[A,b] = A->epsilon
(3) M[B,a] = B->epsilon
(4) M[B,c] = blank

This matches option A.

solution.result: Option A: (1) S->AaAb, (2) A->epsilon, (3) B->epsilon, (4) blank

questions.formula_note: LL(1) table construction using FIRST and FOLLOW sets: M[A,t] = A->alpha when t in FIRST(alpha), or when epsilon in FIRST(alpha) and t in FOLLOW(A).

questions.question:
Consider the context-free grammar where E is the starting symbol, the set of terminals is {d, +, *}, and the set of non-terminals is {E}:

E -> E + E | E * E | d

For the terminal string d + d * d + d, how many parse trees are possible?

A. 5
B. 4
C. 3
D. 2
questions.to_find: The number of distinct parse trees for the string d + d * d + d under the ambiguous grammar E -> E+E | E*E | d

understand.plain: The grammar E -> E+E | E*E | d is ambiguous, meaning a given string can have more than one parse tree. For the string d+d*d+d, we need to count all distinct parse trees. The ambiguity arises because '+' and '*' have no defined precedence or associativity in this grammar. Each different way of parenthesizing d+d*d+d gives a different parse tree.

keywords.explain: A grammar is ambiguous if there exists at least one string that has more than one distinct parse tree (or equivalently, more than one leftmost/rightmost derivation).
keywords.example: E -> E+E | E*E | d is ambiguous because d+d+d can be parsed as (d+d)+d or d+(d+d).

keywords.explain: A rooted, ordered tree that represents the syntactic structure of a string according to a grammar. Each internal node is a non-terminal; each leaf is a terminal.
keywords.example: For E -> E+E with input d+d: root is E, left child E->d, right child E->d, with + in between.

keywords.explain: The nth Catalan number C(n) counts the number of ways to fully parenthesize n+1 items (like a sequence of operators). C(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1).
keywords.example: For 3 operators (4 operands), C(3) = 5, giving 5 distinct parenthesizations.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="480" height="260" viewBox="0 0 480 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x="240" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">5 parse trees for d+d*d+d</text><rect x="10" y="45" width="88" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="54" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">((d+d)*d)+d</text><rect x="106" y="45" width="88" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="150" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(d+(d*d))+d</text><rect x="202" y="45" width="88" height="30" rx="4" class="hi"/><text x="246" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">d+((d*d)+d)</text><rect x="298" y="45" width="88" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="342" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">d+(d*(d+d))</text><rect x="394" y="45" width="88" height="30" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="438" y="65" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(d+d)*(d+d)</text><text x="240" y="110" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">String: d + d * d + d  (3 operators, 4 operands)</text><text x="240" y="135" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Catalan number C(3) = 5</text><text x="240" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">But here operators are not all the same type.</text><text x="240" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Each distinct binary tree structure over 4 leaves</text><text x="240" y="210" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">gives a different parse tree = 5 total.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five distinct parse trees shown as parenthesizations of d+d*d+d: ((d+d)*d)+d, (d+(d*d))+d, d+((d*d)+d), d+(d*(d+d)), (d+d)*(d+d). The grammar is ambiguous so all 5 are valid.

given.aim: Count the number of distinct parse trees for the string d+d*d+d in the ambiguous grammar E -> E+E | E*E | d

terms.meaning: 4 operands (d) connected by 3 binary operators (+, *, +) — a sequence of 4 d's with 3 operators
terms.example: Like counting parenthesizations of a*b*c*d but with mixed operators
terms.connects: With 4 operands and 3 binary operators, total distinct binary trees = Catalan(3) = 5

terms.meaning: The number of distinct full binary trees with 4 leaves (equivalently, ways to parenthesize 4 operands) is the 3rd Catalan number = 5
terms.connects: Since the grammar E->E op E has no precedence/associativity, each binary tree structure is valid

terms.meaning: In this grammar, + and * have the same 'priority' and can associate either way, making all 5 parenthesizations valid parse trees
terms.connects: In a normal grammar with precedence, some would be eliminated; here all 5 remain valid

given.plan: The string d+d*d+d has 4 operand tokens (d) and 3 operator tokens. The number of distinct binary tree structures (parse trees) with n+1 leaves is the nth Catalan number C(n). For n=3: C(3) = 5. Since the grammar has no precedence or associativity, all 5 parenthesizations are valid.

steps.title: Identify the structure: 4 operands, 3 operators
steps.apply:
The string d+d*d+d consists of 4 d's (operands) connected by 3 binary operators (+, *, +).
The grammar E -> E op E is a binary operator grammar. In this grammar, any way of inserting parentheses to make the expression binary is a valid parse tree.
Number of distinct full binary trees with n internal nodes (= n+1 leaves) = C(n), the nth Catalan number.
Here we have 3 operators = 3 internal nodes, so we use C(3).
C(3) = (1/4) * C(6,3) = (1/4) * 20 = 5.
steps.note: The 5 distinct binary trees correspond to 5 different ways to parenthesize 4 operands with 3 binary operators.

steps.title: Enumerate all 5 parse trees explicitly
steps.apply:
All 5 distinct parenthesizations of d op1 d op2 d op3 d (where op1=+, op2=*, op3=+):

1. ((d + d) * d) + d   [split at op3, left subtree splits at op2, which splits at op1]
2. (d + (d * d)) + d   [split at op3, left subtree splits at op1]
3. (d + d) * (d + d)   [split at op2, left splits at op1, right splits at op3]
4. d + ((d * d) + d)   [split at op1, right subtree splits at op3, which splits at op2]
5. d + (d * (d + d))   [split at op1, right subtree splits at op2]

All 5 parenthesizations are syntactically valid under the grammar E->E+E|E*E|d.
steps.note: Note that the tree shape depends on where we split E->E op E at each level. The split points determine both the tree structure and which operator becomes the root at each level.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply:
Total distinct parse trees = C(3) = 5.
This matches option A.

solution.result: 5 distinct parse trees are possible. Answer: A. 5

questions.formula_note: Catalan number C(n) = C(2n,n)/(n+1) counts the number of distinct parse trees for a string of n+1 operands under a fully ambiguous binary operator grammar. For n=3, C(3) = 5.
